EDCS-22500082.summary: A funerary inscription where Hermes dedicates a monument to his wife Aemilia and their sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500084.translation: Quintus Plautius Elenchus, freedman of Quintus, son of Publius, son of Tiberius, for Nigella, his most dutiful freedwoman.
EDCS-22500084.summary: This inscription records the dedication of Quintus Plautius Elenchus, a freedman, to Nigella, his freedwoman, suggesting a personal dedication within the Roman social structure.

EDCS-22500089.translation: The council and people of Cornus decreed that Quintus Sergius Quadratus, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, Roman knight, patron elected by the most distinguished order of the Cornensians for his merits conferred upon the colonists, should have this erected by the collection of funds; the legates Cassius Honor[---] managed the proceedings.
EDCS-22500089.summary: This inscription records the decision by the council and people of Cornus to erect a monument for Quintus Sergius Quadratus, a Roman knight and patron, recognizing his merits to the colonists, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500091.translation: In the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus. Marcus Cominius Crescens, son of Marcus, of the tribe Quirina, priest of the city of Cornus, priest of the province of Sardinia, admitted by the most distinguished order of the Karalitani, by consent of the province of Sardinia, pontifex, IIvvir of the city of [---]tunei[---]no, a citizen, an eques [---], due to his munificence, the college [---]arrius Iscinius [---] SS.
EDCS-22500091.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the career of Marcus Cominius Crescens, detailing his priesthoods in Cornus and Sardinia, and his high status as a Roman knight and IIvvir, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500093.translation: To the departed spirits of Agais [---]
EDCS-22500093.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to 'Agais' and others, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500095.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Critonius Hermes made this for his daughter.
EDCS-22500095.summary: A funerary dedication made by Critonius Hermes for his daughter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500111.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus, who was deserving. He lived 55 years.
EDCS-22500111.summary: A funerary dedication for Quintus, who lived 55 years, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500115.translation: To [my] most sweet son. Antonius Faustus and Iunia Victoria, most unhappy parents, [dedicate this]. He lived 13 years.
EDCS-22500115.summary: A funerary dedication from heartbroken parents, Antonius Faustus and Iunia Victoria, for their son who died at age 13, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500116.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription, likely for an unhappy mother, with illegible details and date.

EDCS-22500120.translation: Marcus Ulpius Victor, an excellent man, procurator of our Emperor, prefect of the province of Sardinia, restored the Temple of Fortuna and the basilica with its tribunal and columns, which had collapsed from age, at the expense of the public treasury, under the supervision of Lucius Magninius Fulvianus, military tribune, curator of the public affairs.
EDCS-22500120.summary: A dedication recording the restoration of a temple and basilica in Sardinia by Marcus Ulpius Victor, a high-ranking imperial official, likely in the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500122.translation: To Isis. Quintus Fufius Proculus and Quintus Fufius Celsus made this temple from the ground up.
EDCS-22500122.summary: A dedication to the goddess Isis, recording the construction of a temple from its foundations by Quintus Fufius Proculus and Quintus Fufius Celsus, likely during the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500124.translation: To our most provident and most brave lord, Valerius Licinianus Licinius, perpetual and always Augustus. Titus Septimius Ianuarius, a most distinguished man, governor of the province of Sardinia, devoted to his divine power and majesty.
EDCS-22500124.summary: A dedication to Emperor Valerius Licinianus Licinius (likely Licinius I) by Titus Septimius Ianuarius, governor of Sardinia, demonstrating imperial loyalty, probably in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-22500125.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Statia Magna, daughter of Publius, of Verona, to his dearest and incomparable, most holy wife. She lived 28 years, 3 months, and 3 days. Marcianus, freedman of Augustus, a registrar of the public land surveyors of Turritana and Tarrensis, made this for her, who deserved well.
EDCS-22500125.summary: A funerary inscription for Statia Magna, dedicated by her husband Marcianus, an imperial freedman and registrar, detailing her age and his devotion, likely from Roman North Africa in the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500127.translation: To Quintus Allius Pudentillus, son of Quintus, of the Colline tribe, an augur and minister of the Lares of the Emperors, from money collected.
EDCS-22500127.summary: A dedication to Quintus Allius Pudentillus, an augur and minister, funded by a collection, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500128.translation: Titus Flavius Iustinus, as duovir, who promised an enlargement beyond 35,000 sesterces for the quinquennial office, made the lake from the foundations with his own money and at his own expense, and brought water into it.
EDCS-22500128.summary: An inscription by Titus Flavius Iustinus, a local magistrate (duovir), detailing his funding of a lake and its water supply as part of his public service, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500130.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iuvenalis, son of Marcus Julius Genialis, made this for Cerdon Hermerotis, from Ostia.
EDCS-22500130.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Iuvenalis for Cerdon Hermerotis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500131.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Proculus the farmer lived 35 years. His wife made this for him, a well-deserving husband.
EDCS-22500131.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Proculus, a farmer, and was erected by his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500133.translation: Crescens placed this for Tiberius Claudius Arogo, well-deserving.
EDCS-22500133.summary: This funerary inscription records Crescens setting up a monument for Tiberius Claudius Arogo, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500135.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Pampula lived 41 years, 5 months, and 10 days. Her sons made this for their dearest mother.
EDCS-22500135.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Iulia Pampula and was erected by her sons, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500137.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lollia Philomonice, who lived two years and five months. Her parents made this for their innocent daughter.
EDCS-22500137.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Lollia Philomonice and was erected by her parents, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500138.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arrius Saturninus, her husband, made this for Lucretia Venusta, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-22500138.summary: This funerary inscription records Arrius Saturninus setting up a monument for his wife Lucretia Venusta, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500144.translation: [This monument was erected for...] Mar[...], who lived X[...]. M[...] made [it].
EDCS-22500144.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the lifespan of an individual and who erected the monument, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500147.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Claudius Amarantus, the father, and Cornelia Venusta, the mother, made this for their most dutiful daughter, Cornelia Tibulles, who lived 23 years.
EDCS-22500147.summary: This funerary inscription records Claudius Amarantus and Cornelia Venusta erecting a monument for their daughter Cornelia Tibulles, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500148.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Maximus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus perpetuo semper Augustus. Titus Septimius Ianuarius, a most distinguished man, governor of the province of Sardinia, devoted to his majesty, [dedicated this].
EDCS-22500148.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Titus Septimius Ianuarius, governor of Sardinia, to Emperor Constantine the Great, likely dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-22500150.translation: [He lived] 56 years, served [in the military] 19 years. Gnaeus Faustinius Felix, his heir, erected [this monument for him, a well-deserving man].
EDCS-22500150.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan and military service of an individual, with the monument erected by his heir, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-22500151.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Calpurnius Venerianus lived 60 years, [3] months. Calpurnia Valeriana made this for her most excellent father.
EDCS-22500151.summary: A funerary epitaph for Calpurnius Venerianus, dedicated by his daughter Calpurnia Valeriana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500152.translation: To the departed spirits. Tiberius Claudius Diorus, freedman of Augustus, [inscribed this for] F[...]
EDCS-22500152.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Diorus, a freedman of an emperor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500153.translation: To the departed spirits. For Claudia Callistes, from Claudia Pythias, freedwoman of Augustus, Acteniana, her most beloved daughter, who lived 21 years, 10 months, 14 days.
EDCS-22500153.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Callistes, dedicated by her mother Claudia Pythias, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500154.translation: Cursius, son of Costinus, aged 20, and his mother, died on the same day. They are buried here.
EDCS-22500154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cursius and his mother who died on the same day, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500157.translation: Tiberius Claudius Actes, his freedman, made this for Hospita Acraba, his wife, who lived 30 years; she is buried here, [along with] himself, his family, and descendants.
EDCS-22500157.summary: A funerary epitaph for Hospita Acraba, dedicated by her husband Tiberius Claudius Actes, also making provisions for himself and descendants, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500158.translation: For Maria Zoili, daughter of Zoilus.
EDCS-22500158.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Maria Zoili, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500159.translation: To the departed spirits. [---]trius Pau[---], lived [---] years. He is buried here.
EDCS-22500159.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500160.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for a couple, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500161.translation: For Valeria Nispenina, his well-deserving, sweetest wife. Her husband Pribatio and her most devoted son Valentinus made this for their wife and mother. She lived 55 years, mourned for 13 days.
EDCS-22500161.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Nispenina, dedicated by her husband and son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500163.translation: For her most sweet and innocent husband Aurelius Antoninus, who lived 50 years, a veteran, well-deserving of his country. Valeria, his widow, with their four children, made this.
EDCS-22500163.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Antoninus, dedicated by his widow Valeria and their four children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500165.translation: To my wife, daughter of Marcus, for my wife, he made [this]. She lived for 35 years.
EDCS-22500165.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman, likely set up by her husband, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500169.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to Bithia, corrupted by age, under the care of Marcus Ulpius Victor, his procurator, an excellent man.
EDCS-22500169.summary: This is a road restoration inscription by Emperor Philip the Arab, detailing the emperor and the official in charge of the work, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500170.translation: ...father of the country, proconsul, and Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, son of our lord Philippus Augustus, restored the road which leads from Nora to Bithia, corrupted by age.
EDCS-22500170.summary: This inscription records the joint restoration of a road by Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Philip the Younger, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500172.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to Carales, corrupted by age, under the care of Marcus Ulpius Victor, his procurator, an excellent man.
EDCS-22500172.summary: This is a road restoration inscription by Emperor Philip the Arab, detailing the emperor and the official in charge of the work, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500173.translation: The Emperor Caesar [Maximianus?] Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power V, consul II, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to Kara libus, corrupted by age, under the care of Marcus Cal[...], procurator of the province of Sardinia, an excellent man.
EDCS-22500173.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by an emperor, likely Maximianus, and the procurator of Sardinia, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-22500778.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul III, father of the country, proconsul, and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, son of our lord Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power V, consul II, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to Karalis, corrupted by age, under the care of Mar[c]us O[lpius? V]ictor[ia?].
EDCS-22500778.summary: This inscription records the joint road restoration by Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Philip the Younger, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500782.translation: The Emperor Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul II, imperator designated III, father of the country, to Secundus [...], proconsul.
EDCS-22500782.summary: This is a dedication inscription by Emperor Vespasian to Secundus, a proconsul, dating to the 1st century CE.

EDCS-22500790.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, and Marcus Aurelius Numerianus, most noble Caesar, under the care of Marcus Aelius Vitalis, an excellent man, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-22500790.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Carus, Carinus, and Numerian by the governor of Sardinia, Marcus Aelius Vitalis, dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-22500792.translation: 131 miles. Flavius Octavianus, governor of the province of Sardinia, devoted to the numen and majesty of our lord Flavius Dalmatius, most blessed Augustus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-22500792.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording a distance and dedicated by Flavius Octavianus to the emperor Dalmatius, dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-22500794.translation: 42 miles. The Emperor Caesar [Maximinus?] Felix Augustus Maximinus [...], holder of tribunician power II, pontifex maximus, restored the road which leads to Turris [...], corrupted by age, under the care of [...].
EDCS-22500794.summary: This is a road restoration inscription by Emperor Maximinus, detailing the emperor and the official in charge, dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-22500795.translation: Imperator Caesar [---] Pius, pontifex maximus, [---]nicus [---], holding tribunician power for the third time, imperator, [---]O consul, father of the country, [---] nobilissimus [---] IVRE [---] E [---].
EDCS-22500795.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording honors or titles bestowed upon an emperor, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22500796.translation: Imperator Caesar [---]R, pontifex maximus, designated consul, imperator for the first time, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Karalibus to Turres, which had been damaged by age, under the supervision of his procurator for the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-22500796.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by an emperor, likely Vespasian or one of his successors, under the supervision of a procurator in Sardinia, dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-22500798.translation: To our lord Julius Dalmaticus, most blessed Caesar, Flavius Octavianus, illustrious man, president of the province of Sardinia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-22500798.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Julius Dalmaticus, a high-ranking official, by Flavius Octavianus, the governor of Sardinia, likely dating to the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22500799.translation: Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus [---] M [---] M [---] which [---] from Turre Karal(ibus) ducit [---] SER [---] R [---] II DAM [---] V [---] under his procurator.
EDCS-22500799.summary: This fragmentary inscription names Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla and likely records the restoration of a road in Sardinia, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22500800.translation: 55 miles from Turre. Imperator Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the fifth time, imperator for the thirteenth time, father of the country, consul for the fifth time, designated consul for the sixth time, censor, repaired and restored [---] under Sextus Subrius Dexter, procurator and prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-22500800.summary: This inscription records the repair and restoration of a road by Emperor Vespasian, measuring 55 miles, under the procurator Sextus Subrius Dexter in Sardinia, dating to c. 74-75 AD.

EDCS-22500801.translation: 56 miles from Turre. Imperator Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the fifth time, imperator for the thirteenth time, father of the country, consul for the fifth time, designated consul for the sixth time, censor, repaired and restored [---] under Sextus Subrio Dextro, procurator and prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-22500801.summary: This inscription records the repair and restoration of a road by Emperor Vespasian, measuring 56 miles, under the procurator Sextus Subrius Dexter in Sardinia, dating to c. 74-75 AD.

EDCS-22500802.translation: 56 miles. Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus [---] [---]ris Karalis leads, which had been damaged by age, they restored, under the supervision of Marcus [D]omitius [---], his procurator.
EDCS-22500802.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Karalis by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, under the supervision of the procurator Marcus Domitius, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-22500804.translation: 165 miles. Imperator Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Karalibus to Olbia, which had been damaged by age, under the supervision of Marcus Ulpius Victor, his illustrious procurator.
EDCS-22500804.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Karalibus to Olbia by Emperor Philip the Arab, measuring 165 miles, under the procurator Marcus Ulpius Victor, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-22500805.translation: [---] to Imperator Caesar [---] Valerius Constantius [---] Invictus [---], pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the country [---] [---] [---] ST [---] M [---] under the supervision of [---] Valerius Domitianus, illustrious man, his procurator.
EDCS-22500805.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to an emperor, likely Constantius Chlorus, by his procurator Valerius Domitianus in Sardinia, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-22500807.summary: This very fragmentary inscription likely records an honor or dedication to Emperor Diocletian, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-23101809.translation: From the Atei family of Arretium.
EDCS-23101809.summary: This is a gentilicial and tribal indication, likely referring to a person or family from Arretium (modern Arezzo), dating to the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-23300121.translation: Of Lucilius Rufus, a man of the highest distinction.
EDCS-23300121.summary: This inscription refers to Lucilius Rufus, a distinguished man, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-23400473.translation: To the Divine Shades. Severa lived 40 years. Her mother made this for her deserving daughter. [---]lias(?) [lived] 80 years and deservedly.
EDCS-23400473.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Severa and another individual, erected by Severa's mother, likely dating to the Roman Empire.

EDCS-23400474.translation: To the Divine Shades. Germano Nepotis (?). Soldier, lived 32 years, served 7 years. His mother Fausta made this.
EDCS-23400474.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier named Germano Nepotis (?), erected by his mother Fausta, dating to the Roman Empire.

EDCS-23400477.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Marcus Caecilius [---]. He lived [---] years. Cornelius [---] Cup[---].
EDCS-23400477.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Caecilius and possibly Cornelius, dating to the Roman Empire.

EDCS-23400478.translation: For Vibia Auge, wife of Quintus Porcius Clarus, he made this.
EDCS-23400478.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Quintus Porcius Clarus for his wife Vibia Auge, dating to the Roman Empire.

EDCS-23400479.summary: This is a genitive case indicating ownership or a relationship, likely a name inscription from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-23400480.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner's name or a dedication, possibly from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-23400482.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner's name or a dedication, possibly from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-23900192.summary: This inscription records the name of Quintus Rusticius, likely a tomb inscription from the Roman period.

EDCS-28200077.translation: 58 miles from Caralis. Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, father of the country, emperor for the 11th time, consul designate for the 4th time, ordered [this] for Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-28200077.summary: This inscription, likely from the reign of Claudius (41-54 CE), records a road milestone ordered by the emperor for a prefect of Sardinia.

EDCS-28300240.translation: Peace// A good man lives in peace// Peace// Bonus [made this]. In peace, Bonus. Amen, peace.
EDCS-28300240.summary: This bilingual inscription in Latin and Hebrew, with a mix of Greek lettering for 'Amen', appears to be a funerary epitaph with religious sentiments, possibly from late antiquity.

EDCS-28300241.translation: Peace upon [restored] Amen// Bonus in peace [restored] Bonus// Amen.
EDCS-28300241.summary: This inscription, primarily in Aramaic and Latin, seems to be a funerary text, possibly a dedication or epitaph, from late antiquity.

EDCS-28300243.translation: Peon Geta the old man has fallen asleep here in peace. His sleeping is his remembrance, and if anyone vexes him, the God of Israel will be his avenger forever.
EDCS-28300243.summary: This is a funerary inscription from late antiquity, possibly dating to the 4th century CE, with a Latin text expressing religious beliefs about death and divine retribution.

EDCS-29600330.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine [Augustus], father of the country, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 36th time, while Titus Pomponius Proculus served as imperial legate, [ordered this]. 10 [miles].
EDCS-29600330.summary: This inscription, likely from the reign of Diocletian (284-305 CE), records a road milestone indicating distance and mentioning an imperial legate.

EDCS-29600348.translation: 150 paces [plus 3] miles. For the Emperors Caesars Marcus Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Romulus, the most noble man, the road which leads from Karales to Olbia, under the care of Cornelius Fortunatianus, governor of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-29600348.summary: This inscription, likely from the early 4th century CE, records a road construction or repair project in Sardinia commissioned by the emperors Maxentius and his son Romulus, overseen by the provincial governor.

EDCS-30000435.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner's name or a dedication, possibly from the Roman period.

EDCS-30000436.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Licinius Felix lived 10 years, 6 months, and 9 days. Calpurnia Restituta made this for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-30000436.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Licinius Felix, recording his age at death and dedicated by his mother, Calpurnia Restituta, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000437.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Stabius Paulinus made this for his dearest and well-deserving wife, Calpurnia Romula, who lived 65 years. And for himself, Marcus Stabius Paulinus, who lived 67 years, for his descendants.
EDCS-30000437.summary: A funerary inscription for Calpurnia Romula and Marcus Stabius Paulinus, erected by Paulinus for his wife and himself and their descendants, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000438.translation: To the departed spirits. Cornelia Iunilla made this for her dearest husband, Marcus Stabius Paulinianus, who lived 48 years. To the departed spirits. Cornelia Iunilla, who lived 67 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-30000438.summary: A tombstone with a double dedication, first for Marcus Stabius Paulinianus, then for his wife Cornelia Iunilla, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000439.translation: To the departed spirits. Scribonia Primitiva, lived 55 years, well-deserving. To the departed spirits. Marcus Insteius Ulbienses, lived 21 years and 6 months, his father made this for his well-deserving son and master. To the departed spirits. Marcus Stabius Mercurinus, lived 35 years, his patroness made this for her well-deserving freedman.
EDCS-30000439.summary: A complex funerary monument with three distinct inscriptions for Scribonia Primitiva, Marcus Insteius Ulbienses, and Marcus Stabius Mercurinus, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000440.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Iulius Calandius, lived 11 years and 5 months. His father made this for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-30000440.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Iulius Calandius, recording his young age and dedicated by his father, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000441.translation: To Gaius Voluscius Festus. His son, the child Voluscius, made this.
EDCS-30000441.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Voluscius Festus, dedicated by his young son, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000442.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Valerius Primitivus, Marcus Valerius Secundinus, for his son, who lived 38 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-30000442.summary: A tombstone for a son of Marcus Valerius Primitivus and Marcus Valerius Secundinus, recording his age and dedication, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000444.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Flora made this for her excellent freedman, Tiberius Claudius Apollonius, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-30000444.summary: A funerary inscription for Tiberius Claudius Apollonius, dedicated by his patroness Claudia Flora, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000449.translation: To the departed spirits. Silvanus, lived [age illegible]. His grandsons made this for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-30000449.summary: A tombstone for Silvanus, dedicated by his grandsons, with a partially illegible age, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000450.translation: To the departed spirits. His wife made this for the most unfortunate youth Sonfrontus, who lived 36 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-30000450.summary: A tombstone for Sonfrontus, recording his young age and dedication by his wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30000452.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul, at the decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-30000452.summary: This inscription records a public work funded by the local council and built at public expense, dedicated to Emperor Valerian in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-30000454.translation: Four Roman miles. To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, and to Gaius Quintus Herennius Etruscus.
EDCS-30000454.summary: This inscription marks a distance of four Roman miles and is dedicated to Emperors Decius and Hostilian, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-30000455.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Valerius Torbenius the younger lived 27 years. Plautia Acutia, his mother, made this.
EDCS-30000455.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Valerius Torbenius the younger, set up by his mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30400549.translation: From the workshops of Domitia Lucilla.
EDCS-30400549.summary: This inscription identifies the origin of the pottery as being from the workshops of Domitia Lucilla, a known ceramic producer of the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31500018.translation: Thirty-three Roman miles. The Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, and Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, prince of youth, restored the road which leads from Turris to Carales, which had been corrupted by age, under the supervision of Publius Aelius Valens, his excellent procurator.
EDCS-31500018.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Turris and Carales, commissioned by Emperors Philip the Elder and his son, under the supervision of a procurator, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500019.translation: Thirty-three Roman miles. To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius [Maxim]us Pius Felix Invictus Augustus [pontifex] maximus, imperator for the second time, consul, father of the country, proconsul, under the supervision of Lucius Septimius Leonticus, an excellent man, procurator [of the province] of Sardinia.
EDCS-31500019.summary: This inscription marks a distance of thirty-three Roman miles and records the restoration of a road in Sardinia, under the supervision of a procurator during the reign of Emperor Claudius Gothicus (c. 268-270 CE).

EDCS-31700053.translation: One hundred and sixty-two Roman miles. [---]mius Pius Felix, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, [restored the road which] leads from Karales to Olbia, which had been corrupted by age, under the supervision of Marcus [---].
EDCS-31700053.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Karales and Olbia, attributed to a pontifex maximus and supervised by Marcus, likely dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-31700054.translation: One hundred and sixty-five Roman miles. Our lord Emperor Caesar Domitianus [Aurelianus] restored [the road which] leads from Karales to Olbia, corrupted by age. P[---]tius, a most distinguished man, praeses [of the province] of Sardinia.
EDCS-31700054.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Karales and Olbia by Emperor Aurelian, under the administration of the governor P[---]tius, dating to c. 270-275 CE.

EDCS-31700055.translation: Boundary marker. Between the Maltamones, of Censorius Secundinus, a man of highest distinction, and the Semilitenses, of Quarta, a most honorable woman, the boundary markers were uprooted in due time and because they were located [---]
EDCS-31700055.summary: This inscription marks a boundary dispute between two groups, referencing local officials and landowners, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31700064.translation: To Aurelius, from the liburna Salus Augusta, aged 60 years, with 30 years of service. He is buried here.
EDCS-31700064.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius, a soldier who served for 30 years and died at the age of 60, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31700065.translation: 160 miles. For our lord Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, for the road which leads from Cagliari to Olbia, ruined by age, having been restored by Septimius Nigerinus, vir perfectissimus, his procurator, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-31700065.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Karalis (Cagliari) and Olbia, undertaken by the procurator Septimius Nigerinus under Emperor Aurelian (c. 270-275 AD).

EDCS-31700066.translation: 160 miles. For our lord Emperor Caesar Valerius Licinius Valerius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, for the road which leads from Cagliari to Olbia, consumed by age, having been restored by Lucius Messius Rusticus, vir perfectissimus, president of the province of Sardinia, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-31700066.summary: This inscription documents the restoration of a road between Karalis (Cagliari) and Olbia, executed by Lucius Messius Rusticus, governor of Sardinia, during the reign of Emperor Licinius (c. 308-324 AD).

EDCS-31700079.translation: [--] miles. For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus Nobilissimus Caesar, the road which leads from Cagliari to Olbia, corrupted by age, was restored by Marcus Aelius Vitalis, vir perfectissimus, president of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-31700079.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Karalis (Cagliari) and Olbia, attributed to Marcus Aelius Vitalis, governor of Sardinia, under Emperor Carinus (c. 283-285 AD).

EDCS-31700084.translation: From the larger terracotta molds of our Caesar, a brickwork, by Quintus Perusius Pudens.
EDCS-31700084.summary: This is a maker's mark from terracotta tiles, indicating production from the larger molds belonging to the emperor, by Quintus Perusius Pudens.

EDCS-31700100.translation: From the larger terracotta molds of our Caesar, a brickwork, by Quintus Perusius Pudens.
EDCS-31700100.summary: This is a maker's mark from terracotta tiles, indicating production from the larger molds belonging to the emperor, by Quintus Perusius Pudens.

EDCS-33600035.translation: For our lord Publius Licinius Cornelius Saloninus Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix Nobilissimus Caesar.
EDCS-33600035.summary: This fragment appears to be part of a dedication or honorific inscription for the young Caesar Saloninus Valerianus (c. 253-260 AD).

EDCS-33600036.translation: And for Gaius Marcius Flavius and Cornelia Gallonia, Augusta, wife of our lord Publius Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, of their entire Divine House, for Fortune Returning. [For the safety and return and victory of Emperor Caesar Publi Licinius Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix and for Gaius Marcius Flavius and Cornelia Gallonia Augusta, wife of our lord Publius Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, of their entire Divine House, for Fortune Returning.]
EDCS-33600036.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a dedication for the safety and prosperity of Emperor Valerian I and his family, including Empress Cornelia Gallonia, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-33600037.translation: Orcoeta, son of Bihonis(?), a soldier from the cohort of Aquitanians, aged 21 years. Here lies.
EDCS-33600037.summary: This is a tombstone for Orcoeta, a soldier of Dacian origin serving in the cohort of Aquitanians, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34000175.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Servilius Nonianus and Gaius Cestius. Gaius Cuspius Felix, priest of Bubastis, a sacred dedication.
EDCS-34000175.summary: This inscription records a sacred offering made by Gaius Cuspius Felix, a priest of the goddess Bubastis, during the consulship of Servilius Nonianus and Cestius in 4 AD.

EDCS-34100467.translation: To the divine spirits. For Lucius Scaentius Valens, a soldier from the Praetorian fleet of Misenum, century of Socellus, son of Chilo. He served for 27 years, lived for 47 years. Of Bassus nationality. Eutycha made this for her most excellent and deserving patron. Lucius Calpurnius Firmus took care of the arrangements.
EDCS-34100467.summary: This tombstone honors Lucius Scaentius Valens, a sailor from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, who served for 27 years and lived to 47, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700003.translation: 166 miles. To our lord the Emperor, Pious, Fortunate...
EDCS-34700003.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, records a distance of 166 miles dedicated to an emperor.

EDCS-34700078.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Turranius Celer, soldier from the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, a Dalmatian by birth, lived 40 years, served 23 years. Quintus Naevius Aquila, centurion, saw to this.
EDCS-34700078.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and service of Lucius Turranius Celer, a soldier from the Misenum fleet, prepared by a centurion, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700080.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Chrysus Venustus, who lived 26 years and 2 months. Mutilia Certa made this for her son.
EDCS-34700080.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Lucius Chrysus Venustus and was dedicated by his mother, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700081.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Euhodus, a well-deserving slave, who lived 25 years, 5 months, and 27 days. Flavius Asiaticus Albinovanus made this.
EDCS-34700081.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Euhodus, a slave, and was dedicated by Flavius Asiaticus Albinovanus, likely from the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-34700082.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gessia Galla, who lived 60 years. For her most beloved mother by her sons.
EDCS-34700082.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Gessia Galla and was dedicated by her sons, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700083.translation: Helios Secunda made this for her well-deserving husband. He lived 35 years.
EDCS-34700083.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Helios Secunda's husband, who lived 35 years, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700084.translation: Marcus Herennius Victor, Egnatia Iunia, daughter of Gaius, for Gaius Herennius Iunior, son of Marcus, for Herennia Victrix, daughter of Marcus. For her sons, for herself, and for their descendants.
EDCS-34700084.summary: This funerary epitaph records a family burial plot established by Marcus Herennius Victor and Egnatia Iunia for their children and descendants, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700088.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Magnia Lucretia, who lived 30 years, a good and incomparable wife. Her husband made this.
EDCS-34700088.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Magnia Lucretia, dedicated by her husband, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700089.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Valerius Germanus, a soldier with 26 years of service. His father made this for him, a well-deserving soldier.
EDCS-34700089.summary: This funerary epitaph records the military service of Marcus Valerius Germanus, dedicated by his father, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700090.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Valerius lived 80 years. For his father, a well-deserving father, his sons made this.
EDCS-34700090.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Marcus Valerius, dedicated by his sons, likely from the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-34700091.translation: To the divine shades. To Valeria Silvana, who lived 53 years, a well-deserving mother, from her sons.
EDCS-34700091.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Valeria Silvana by her sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700092.translation: To the divine shades. Lucius Valerius Marcellinus lived 27 years and...
EDCS-34700092.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Marcellinus, who died at the age of 27, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700094.translation: To the divine shades. Iulia Valeria, also called Ginsburg, lived 56 years.
EDCS-34700094.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Valeria, also known as Ginsburg, who lived to be 56, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700095.translation: To the divine shades. Valeria Urelio lived 35 years.
EDCS-34700095.summary: A funerary inscription for Valeria Urelio, who died at the age of 35, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700097.translation: To the divine shades. Hilara lived 35 years, 15 days, and 9 hours. To her mother, well-deserving, from her sons (?).
EDCS-34700097.summary: A funerary inscription for Hilara, who lived 35 years, 15 days, and 9 hours, with a dedication potentially from her sons to her, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700099.translation: Fulvila. Here lies her son, 19 years of age.
EDCS-34700099.summary: A funerary inscription for the son of Fulvila, aged 19, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700100.translation: ... Octavius (?), who lived 26 years... Domitia Fusca to her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-34700100.summary: A funerary inscription for an unnamed man, possibly Octavius, who lived 26 years, dedicated by Domitia Fusca, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700101.translation: Pertius, son of Cursus, 25 years of age. Here lies.
EDCS-34700101.summary: A funerary inscription for Pertius, son of Cursus, who died at the age of 25, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700102.translation: To the divine shades. To Valeria [---]iae L[---].
EDCS-34700102.summary: A damaged funerary inscription likely dedicated to a woman named Valeria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700103.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fifth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, son of our lord Philippus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fifth time, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road which leads from Nora to [Bitiae], corrupted by age, under the supervision of Publius Aelius Valens, their procurator, a distinguished man, prefect of the province of Sardinia.
EDCS-34700103.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a road between Nora and Bitiae by the Emperors Philip the Elder and Philip the Younger, during the reign of Philip the Elder and under the administration of procurator Publius Aelius Valens, dated to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-34700104.translation: 7 miles. Under Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Caro Pius Felix Invictus the Elder, Pontiff, Master of Tribunician Power, Consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, and Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar, the road which leads from Norica to Dacia, ruined by age, [was restored].
EDCS-34700104.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road between Norica and Dacia under the emperors Carus and Carinus, likely in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34700108.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely mentioning a governor named Aelius, with its original context and date unknown.

EDCS-34700112.translation: 165 miles. Under Emperor Marcus Aurelius Caro Pius Felix Augustus and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus, Pius Felix [Augustus], Pontiff, Master of Tribunician Power, [the road ruined] by age [was restored].
EDCS-34700112.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road under the emperors Carus and Carinus, likely in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34700114.translation: To Aurelius [Augustus] Victorious always, [forever] our lords, they themselves dedicated. [With] good auspices this road which leads from Karalis to Olbia, ruined, [they will restore], under Marcus Aurelius [Marco].
EDCS-34700114.summary: This inscription records the dedication and planned restoration of a road originating from Karalis under the emperor Aurelius, possibly in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34700134.translation: 150 miles. Under Emperor Caesar [...], Valerianus Pius Felici [...], greatest, with tribunician power [...], and [...] and [...] Gallienus [...] Pro[...]ano [...] consul, Publius Licinius [...] most noble Caesars [...], from Karalis [...], under the supervision of [...]
EDCS-34700134.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning the emperors Valerian and Gallienus, possibly recording road construction or repair, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-35200342.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Philete, a slave girl, lived 18 years. Valeria Crescentia made this for her dearest, well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-35200342.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Philete, a slave girl, set up by her mother Valeria Crescentia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-36100355.translation: Doliary work from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, of the workshop of Peduceius Lupus.
EDCS-36100355.summary: This inscription records doliary (barrel-making) work originating from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, attributed to the workshop of Peduceius Lupus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-36100358.translation: Doliary work from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, of Verus Claudius Quinquatralis, under Antoninus IIII and Verus II, consuls.
EDCS-36100358.summary: This inscription records doliary work from Domitia Lucilla's estates by Verus Claudius Quinquatralis, dated to the consulship of Antoninus and Verus, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-36400232.translation: [...] to Asclepius, he willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-36400232.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Asclepius, indicating a vow fulfilled, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39101512.translation: To the Chthonic Gods. Apollonius, a periodic winner in kitharody, dedicated [this] as a freedman of his.
EDCS-39101512.summary: This is a Greek inscription dedicated to the Chthonic Gods by Apollonius, a winner in musical competitions, who was a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-40900153.translation: Victoria Prima lived for 31 years. Mactosius Martialus made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-40900153.summary: A tombstone (epitaph) recording the lifespan of Victoria Prima and commissioned by her husband, Mactosius Martialus.

EDCS-40900155.translation: To Tita Aurelia, deserving of all good things. Munatianus, the heir, [made this].
EDCS-40900155.summary: An epitaph honoring Tita Aurelia, with the heir, Munatianus, identified as the dedicator.

EDCS-43400282.translation: By order of Liber Pater, Marcus Arrecinus Helius, prefect of the Valentinian city, restored this at his own expense.
EDCS-43400282.summary: A dedication recording the restoration of a public building or monument in the Valentinian city by its prefect, Marcus Arrecinus Helius.

EDCS-43600118.inscription_type: carmina, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-43600118.translation: Work begun and offered for the sacred memory of Atilia Pomptilla, blessed daughter of Lucius. Her husband Philip [did this] at his own expense. // Grave is the fate that followed the wife of my unhappy husband even to this point; here I, Atilia, lie, consecrated to my husband's manes. Praying for his life, I was granted the indulgence of the gods to give my life in exchange. Do not cease, fame, for we have earned it. // The temple which you, traveler, often adore, covers the ashes and small bones of Pomptilla. I lie pressed by the Sardinian earth, accompanied by my husband. It is said that for my husband's sake I wished to die. // May your bones, Pomptilla, sprout forth into lilies and the blossoms of fragrant crocus and ageless amaranth, and may your flower bloom white like the many-weeping narcissus and hyacinth, and may time hold your flower among future generations. For when Philip released his last breath from his limbs, placing his soul upon his lips, Pomptilla, standing by her fainting husband, took on his life in place of death. // What a union the god forged, so that Pomptilla died as a ransom for her dear husband, while Philip lived, always praying that his soul might be mingled with his most loving wife's. // We lived twenty-two years joined together; one faith gave us many joys. And since Pomptilla was first taken to the underworld, Lethe said, 'Philip, live in my time.' Now eternal rest and the silent underworld have established this home for us both, because of your piety. // While the sad Pomptilla languished, mourning her husband, she vowed to die for her husband's life. Immediately she seemed to glide into peaceful rest and died. Oh swift, but ill-omened, vows of the gods! To hear these prayers and preserve [my] life for [my] husband, so that that life might perish, which is sweeter than mine. // One of the Fates turned my threads back from being unchangeable, [responding] to my prayers with all [strength?]. The prudent Pomptilla, much-spoken-of, who alone gave up her spirit for her husband. // She lived her life for almost sixty years and two without reproach. The renowned Pomptilla... // ... // ... // ... // The piety of the husband built temples for a great reward. The chaste woman deserved to be honored. For she dedicated herself when her husband was already fading, so that he might live, her worthy husband. // Here Pomptilla lies, embracing the ashes of Philip. The urn of the two is enclosed within these altars, which makes an argument for fame to the living. // Behold, all, the seat of Juno Inferna. By a changed divinity, Pomptilla shines through eternity. // Let Alcestis no longer [speak]... and [the one] who once embraced flame with Capaneus... // ... // Let the much-spoken-of heroines, whom ancient time inscribed among the immortals, be silenced, for in later generations Atilia, before Philip, surpasses them, who prayed for her husband's most unbelievable fate. // Traveler, behold the tomb of Pomptilla, who tasted the sweet end of life for her husband.
EDCS-43600118.summary: A comprehensive funerary monument commemorating Atilia Pomptilla and her husband Philip, detailing her sacrifice of life for his, and including extensive Latin and Greek verses reflecting on their devotion, her piety, and her posthumous fame, possibly dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-44100047.translation: C. Aristius Rufus, IIvir, dedicated this monument as aedile at his own expense.
EDCS-44100047.summary: A public dedication by C. Aristius Rufus, who held the office of IIvir and aedile, funded by himself.

EDCS-44100048.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiburtia Am[--] lived 60 years. Julia Amana made this for her most beloved mother, deserving of all good things.
EDCS-44100048.summary: A tombstone for Tiburtia Am-- who lived 60 years, erected by her devoted daughter Julia Amana.

EDCS-44100049.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vitellia Iuliana lived for 3 years, 1 month. Parents [made this] for their most beloved daughter.
EDCS-44100049.summary: A tombstone for Vitellia Iuliana, who died at the age of 3 years and 1 month, erected by her parents.

EDCS-46600172.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul, by decree of the town councilors, at public expense.
EDCS-46600172.summary: A formal inscription dedicating a public work or building to Emperor Valerian, likely during his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-46600174.translation: Marsyas, from Neapolis, may you make Decimus Ostilius Donatus wretched, mute, and deaf, just as you respond to a human.
EDCS-46600174.summary: A curse tablet invoking the mythical figure Marsyas, asking for the affliction of Decimus Ostilius Donatus with misfortune, muteness, and deafness.

EDCS-48200124.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Annius Serapidorus.

EDCS-48200129.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the ownership or dedication of an object or property, possibly belonging to Marcus Arius Fr... . The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48200131.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the ownership mark or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Caius Cornelius Ursus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48200132.translation: From the workshop of Quintus Sempronius
EDCS-48200132.summary: This inscription indicates the origin or ownership of an object, stating it comes from the workshop of Quintus Sempronius. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48300176.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely recording the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Annius Serapidorus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48300177.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Annius Serapidorus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48300185.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely recording the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Biricus Agathopus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600037.translation: Claudius Capito's freedwoman Fauxtila lived 35 years. Claudius [?] is buried here.
EDCS-48600037.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and age of Fauxtila, freedwoman of Claudius Capito, and mentions the burial of Claudius [?]. It dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48600038.translation: For the health, safety, return, and victory of the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul, and of Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar's son, of the Divine House, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, Minerva, Hope, and Health.
EDCS-48600038.summary: This dedication records prayers for the well-being and success of Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Gaius Valerius Maximus, offered to major Roman deities. It dates to the reign of Maximinus Thrax (235-238 AD).

EDCS-48600053.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Lucius Rasinius Pisanus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600060.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or dedication of an object or property, belonging to Sextus Murrius Festus. The date is indeterminate.

EDCS-48600061.summary: This inscription records the ownership or dedication by Sextus Murrus Pisanus, likely on a private monument.

EDCS-48600062.summary: This inscription records the ownership or dedication by Vilius Natalis, likely on a private monument.

EDCS-48600070.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-48600070.summary: This inscription records something belonging to or decreed in the name of Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600071.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. 225.
EDCS-48600071.summary: This inscription records a quantity of 225 associated with Emperor Hadrian, possibly related to tribute or a measurement from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600072.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 11.
EDCS-48600072.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 11 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600073.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 23.
EDCS-48600073.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 23 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600074.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 29.
EDCS-48600074.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 29 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600075.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-48600075.summary: This inscription records something belonging to or decreed in the name of Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600076.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 29.
EDCS-48600076.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 29 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600077.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus. Tax 26.
EDCS-48600077.summary: This inscription records a tax assessment of 26 units associated with Emperor Hadrian, likely from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-48600078.translation: From the census of Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, 28.
EDCS-48600078.summary: This inscription appears to record a census figure related to Emperor Hadrian, dating to his reign.

EDCS-48600079.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. German(a), mother, and Honorat(us), father, made this for their son, who lived 9 years, a well-deserving child.
EDCS-48600079.summary: A tombstone erected by parents for their young son, Honaritanus, who died at age nine.

EDCS-48600080.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucus(ti)on and his wife Montan(a) made this for their father, Silvanus the tenant farmer, who lived 65 years, 3 months, and 20 days, a well-deserving father.
EDCS-48600080.summary: A tombstone erected by a son and his wife for their father, Silvanus, a tenant farmer, who lived to be 65.

EDCS-51400894.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Coelius Abdi[t]inus made this for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-51400894.summary: A tombstone fragment indicating a father set it up for his son, with the father likely being Gaius Coelius Abditinus.

EDCS-52000074.translation: From Tower LXXVIII. Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, emperor for the 11th time, consul designate for the 4th time, ordered [this] for Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-52000074.summary: An official inscription indicating Emperor Claudius ordered a construction or dedication at Tower 78 for Lucius Aurelius Patroclus, the prefect of Sardinia, likely dating to Claudius's reign (41-54 AD).

EDCS-58700040.translation: Born on the Kalends of April. Baptized on the fourth day before the Nones of April. Here lies Theusebius, the innocent, newly initiated, virgin. He was dear to me, pious, my son, behold, buried. Theusebi, holy, always associated with chastity. He spent twelve months and thirty days plus more in the light of God. Stainless from sin, he was happily laid to rest as a gift. Completed on his birthday and reborn by Christ, Theusebius entered the eternal abode of life, and with the Lord granting a crown, he bears a virgin's state. Rufinus and Severa, parents, for their dearest son. They made this for him well-deserving, in the Lord. He rests in peace, laid to rest on the sixth day before the Nones of May. Here first, my son, here first I dedicated this place as a virgin for a virgin.
EDCS-58700040.summary: A complex Christian funerary inscription by parents Rufinus and Severa for their son Theusebius, detailing his birth and baptism dates, his virtuous life as a virgin, and his death, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-58700045.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-58700045.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to foot soldiers and cavalrymen who served in two cohorts named I Gemina Sardorum et Corsorum and II Gemina Ligurum et Corsorum, who are in Sardinia, after completing twenty-five or more years of service, and were honorably discharged, whose names are inscribed below, to them, their children, and their descendants; and also to those who were unmarried, with the wives whom they would have at the time citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, each one woman. On the third day before the Nones of May, when Titus Didius Secundus and Lucius Publilius Celsus were consuls. For Hannibal son of Tabilatis of the Nur() Alb() tribe, a foot soldier of the II Gemina Ligurum et Corsorum cohort, commanded by Lucius Terentius Serenus, and for his wife Iurina daughter of Tammuga, Sordia, and for their son Sabinus, and for their son Saturninus, and for their daughter Tisarena, and for their daughter Bolgitta, and for their daughter Bonasson. This document was transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed on a wall in Rome behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near the Minerva.
EDCS-58700045.summary: An imperial rescript, likely issued under Trajan (98-117 AD), granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers who completed long service in Sardinia, recorded on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-59500348.translation: For Flavia Publicia, the chief Vestal Virgin, exempt from taxes, in the Portus district, with a fish emblem, belonging to Eudromus of Porphyris.

EDCS-79600257.summary: This is a tombstone for Sextus Elanus Proculus, a naval soldier who served in the Misenum fleet, giving his service and life span.

EDCS-79600260.translation: To the spirits of the departed? To... Ritus? To... a well-deserving person.
EDCS-79600260.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription honoring a deceased individual.

EDCS-80200061.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fint[3 S]ilva/niani lived 40 and X years.
EDCS-80200061.summary: This is a fragmented tombstone recording the age of Fint[3 S]ilviani at the time of death.

EDCS-82100035.translation: Luna, Mars, Venus, Amor // Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus, Rufus // Ianuaria, Pyrrhus, Epidius, Hilarus(?), Secundus.
EDCS-82100035.summary: This inscription lists a series of names, likely representing deities, patrons, and clients or individuals associated with a specific group or location.

EDCS-82800049.translation: X miles from the Tower. Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, Imperator for the 13th time, Father of the Country, Consul for the 5th time, designated Consul for the 6th time, censor, rebuilt and restored. Under Sextus Subrius Dexter, procurator and prefect of Sardinia.
EDCS-82800049.summary: This milestone records the rebuilding and restoration of a road by Emperor Vespasian, noting the distance and the procurator of Sardinia, Sex. Subrius Dexter, likely dating to between 73 and 74 AD.

EDCS-82800054.translation: To the Nymphs. Valeria Modesta, freedwoman of Marcus Valerius Optatus, procurator Augusti and prefect of the province of Sardinia, gave this gift.
EDCS-82800054.summary: This is a dedication to the Nymphs by Valeria Modesta, a freedwoman of the procurator and prefect of Sardinia, Marcus Valerius Optatus.
